title: Translation (geometry)

infobox.value: is a [geometric transformation](/wiki/Geometric_transformation "Geometric transformation") that moves every point of a figure, shape or space by the same [distance](/wiki/Distance_geometry "Distance geometry") in a given [direction](/wiki/Direction_(geometry) "Direction (geometry)"). A translation can also be interpreted as the addition of a constant [vector](/wiki/Vector_space "Vector space") to every point, or as shifting the [origin](/wiki/Origin_(mathematics) "Origin (mathematics)") of the [coordinate system](/wiki/Coordinate_system "Coordinate system"). In a [Euclidean space](/wiki/Euclidean_space "Euclidean space"), any translation is an [isometry](/wiki/Isometry "Isometry").

of ![{\displaystyle A}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7daff47fa58cdfd29dc333def748ff5fa4c923e3) by ![{\displaystyle T}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ec7200acd984a1d3a3d7dc455e262fbe54f7f6e0). The translate of ![{\displaystyle A}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7daff47fa58cdfd29dc333def748ff5fa4c923e3) by ![{\displaystyle T_{\mathbf {v} }}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9caf87fd0b979b2a34fabb83c43f78c3702d6d8b) is often written as ![{\displaystyle A+\mathbf {v} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3b1cbdd363a411f561d1df1d6ece16a792732ebe).

![{\displaystyle \mathbb {T} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9c039979935c00b3b216cbb065999207872677f6), which is isomorphic to the space itself, and a [normal subgroup](/wiki/Normal_subgroup "Normal subgroup") of [Euclidean group](/wiki/Euclidean_group "Euclidean group") ![{\displaystyle E(n)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a8b3b6ec0effe4cf5a6a54bd424fb04984dbe97d). The [quotient group](/wiki/Quotient_group "Quotient group") of ![{\displaystyle E(n)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a8b3b6ec0effe4cf5a6a54bd424fb04984dbe97d) by ![{\displaystyle \mathbb {T} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9c039979935c00b3b216cbb065999207872677f6) is isomorphic to the group of rigid motions which fix a particular origin point, the [orthogonal group](/wiki/Orthogonal_group "Orthogonal group") ![{\displaystyle O(n)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/34109fe397fdcff370079185bfdb65826cb5565a):

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Planar movement within a Euclidean space without rotation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/01/Traslazione_OK.svg/250px-Traslazione_OK.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Traslazione_OK.svg)

A translation moves every point of a figure or a space by the same amount in a given direction.

In [Euclidean geometry](/wiki/Euclidean_geometry "Euclidean geometry"), a **translation** is a [geometric transformation](/wiki/Geometric_transformation "Geometric transformation") that moves every point of a figure, shape or space by the same [distance](/wiki/Distance_geometry "Distance geometry") in a given [direction](/wiki/Direction_(geometry) "Direction (geometry)"). A translation can also be interpreted as the addition of a constant [vector](/wiki/Vector_space "Vector space") to every point, or as shifting the [origin](/wiki/Origin_(mathematics) "Origin (mathematics)") of the [coordinate system](/wiki/Coordinate_system "Coordinate system"). In a [Euclidean space](/wiki/Euclidean_space "Euclidean space"), any translation is an [isometry](/wiki/Isometry "Isometry").

## As a function

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation_(geometry)&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: As a function")]

See also: [Displacement (geometry)](/wiki/Displacement_(geometry) "Displacement (geometry)")

If ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {v} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/35c1866e359fbfd2e0f606c725ba5cc37a5195d6) is a fixed vector, known as the *translation vector*, and ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {p} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/dd73e3862cb92b016721b8c492eadb4e8a577527) is the initial position of some object, then the translation function ![{\displaystyle T_{\mathbf {v} }}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9caf87fd0b979b2a34fabb83c43f78c3702d6d8b) will work as ![{\displaystyle T_{\mathbf {v} }(\mathbf {p} )=\mathbf {p} +\mathbf {v} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d2ef190970526b4786ba110e009049bb934d3015).

If ![{\displaystyle T}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ec7200acd984a1d3a3d7dc455e262fbe54f7f6e0) is a translation, then the [image](/wiki/Image_(mathematics) "Image (mathematics)") of a subset ![{\displaystyle A}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7daff47fa58cdfd29dc333def748ff5fa4c923e3) under the [function](/wiki/Function_(mathematics) "Function (mathematics)") ![{\displaystyle T}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ec7200acd984a1d3a3d7dc455e262fbe54f7f6e0) is the **translate** of ![{\displaystyle A}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7daff47fa58cdfd29dc333def748ff5fa4c923e3) by ![{\displaystyle T}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ec7200acd984a1d3a3d7dc455e262fbe54f7f6e0). The translate of ![{\displaystyle A}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7daff47fa58cdfd29dc333def748ff5fa4c923e3) by ![{\displaystyle T_{\mathbf {v} }}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9caf87fd0b979b2a34fabb83c43f78c3702d6d8b) is often written as ![{\displaystyle A+\mathbf {v} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/3b1cbdd363a411f561d1df1d6ece16a792732ebe).

### Application in classical physics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation_(geometry)&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Application in classical physics")]

In [classical physics](/wiki/Classical_physics "Classical physics"), translational motion is movement that changes the [position](/wiki/Position_(geometry) "Position (geometry)") of an object, as opposed to [rotation](/wiki/Rotation "Rotation"). For example, according to Whittaker:[[1]](#cite_note-Whittaker-1)

> If a body is moved from one position to another, and if the lines joining the initial and final points of each of the points of the body are a set of parallel straight lines of length *ℓ*, so that the orientation of the body in space is unaltered, the displacement is called a *translation parallel to the direction of the lines, through a distance ℓ*.

— [E. T. Whittaker](/wiki/E._T._Whittaker "E. T. Whittaker"), *[A Treatise on the Analytical Dynamics of Particles and Rigid Bodies](/wiki/A_Treatise_on_the_Analytical_Dynamics_of_Particles_and_Rigid_Bodies "A Treatise on the Analytical Dynamics of Particles and Rigid Bodies")*, p. 1

A translation is the operation changing the positions of all points ![{\displaystyle (x,y,z)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/22a8c93372e8f8b6e24d523bd5545aed3430baf4) of an object according to the formula

:   ![{\displaystyle (x,y,z)\to (x+\Delta x,y+\Delta y,z+\Delta z)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e9b242a68ba548eb06d7f4a6c0c96a63cc2270c2)

where ![{\displaystyle (\Delta x,\ \Delta y,\ \Delta z)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a1e88155be8e90e86498eb270fef6fc6636d1c9b) is the same [vector](/wiki/Euclidean_vector "Euclidean vector") for each point of the object. The translation vector ![{\displaystyle (\Delta x,\ \Delta y,\ \Delta z)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a1e88155be8e90e86498eb270fef6fc6636d1c9b) common to all points of the object describes a particular type of [displacement](/wiki/Displacement_(vector) "Displacement (vector)") of the object, usually called a *linear* displacement to distinguish it from displacements involving rotation, called *angular* displacements.

When considering [spacetime](/wiki/Spacetime "Spacetime"), a change of [time](/wiki/Time "Time") coordinate is considered to be a translation.

## As an operator

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation_(geometry)&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: As an operator")]

Main article: [Shift operator](/wiki/Shift_operator "Shift operator")

The [translation operator](/wiki/Shift_operator "Shift operator") turns a function of the original position, ![{\displaystyle f(\mathbf {v} )}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/387ffaa6b5e65940435d2ccc9228c471621a4bb2), into a function of the final position, ![{\displaystyle f(\mathbf {v} +\mathbf {\delta } )}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/645c3deb2646de978c7b8d0c49c6955ae1e410d8). In other words, ![{\displaystyle T_{\mathbf {\delta } }}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/75a6730ac2e36afbbc5c42952ed336bb698195bb) is defined such that ![{\displaystyle T_{\mathbf {\delta } }f(\mathbf {v} )=f(\mathbf {v} +\mathbf {\delta } ).}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/36a613b788721a990e94d2ab4bbfa34d304ff483) This [operator](/wiki/Operator_(mathematics) "Operator (mathematics)") is more abstract than a function, since ![{\displaystyle T_{\mathbf {\delta } }}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/75a6730ac2e36afbbc5c42952ed336bb698195bb) defines a relationship between two functions, rather than the underlying vectors themselves. The translation operator can act on many kinds of functions, such as when the [translation operator acts on a wavefunction](/wiki/Translation_operator_(quantum_mechanics) "Translation operator (quantum mechanics)"), which is studied in the field of quantum mechanics.

## As a group

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation_(geometry)&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: As a group")]

See also: [Translation operator (quantum mechanics) § Translation group](/wiki/Translation_operator_(quantum_mechanics)#Translation_group "Translation operator (quantum mechanics)")

The set of all translations forms the **translation group** ![{\displaystyle \mathbb {T} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9c039979935c00b3b216cbb065999207872677f6), which is isomorphic to the space itself, and a [normal subgroup](/wiki/Normal_subgroup "Normal subgroup") of [Euclidean group](/wiki/Euclidean_group "Euclidean group") ![{\displaystyle E(n)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a8b3b6ec0effe4cf5a6a54bd424fb04984dbe97d). The [quotient group](/wiki/Quotient_group "Quotient group") of ![{\displaystyle E(n)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a8b3b6ec0effe4cf5a6a54bd424fb04984dbe97d) by ![{\displaystyle \mathbb {T} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/9c039979935c00b3b216cbb065999207872677f6) is isomorphic to the group of rigid motions which fix a particular origin point, the [orthogonal group](/wiki/Orthogonal_group "Orthogonal group") ![{\displaystyle O(n)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/34109fe397fdcff370079185bfdb65826cb5565a):

:   ![{\displaystyle E(n)/\mathbb {T} \cong O(n)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0f0cb95c20928b99fbfd6d9f8a34c08c559854d7)

Because translation is [commutative](/wiki/Commutative "Commutative"), the translation group is [abelian](/wiki/Abelian_group "Abelian group"). There are an infinite number of possible translations, so the translation group is an [infinite group](/wiki/Infinite_group "Infinite group").

In the [theory of relativity](/wiki/Theory_of_relativity "Theory of relativity"), due to the treatment of space and time as a single [spacetime](/wiki/Spacetime "Spacetime"), translations can also refer to changes in the [time coordinate](/wiki/Coordinate_time "Coordinate time"). For example, the [Galilean group](/wiki/Galilean_group "Galilean group") and the [Poincaré group](/wiki/Poincar%C3%A9_group "Poincaré group") include translations with respect to time.

### Lattice groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation_(geometry)&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Lattice groups")]

Main article: [Lattice (group)](/wiki/Lattice_(group) "Lattice (group)")

One kind of [subgroup](/wiki/Subgroup "Subgroup") of the three-dimensional translation group are the [lattice groups](/wiki/Lattice_(group) "Lattice (group)"), which are [infinite groups](/wiki/Infinite_group "Infinite group"), but unlike the translation groups, are [finitely generated](/wiki/Finitely_generated_group "Finitely generated group"). That is, a finite [generating set](/wiki/Generating_set_of_a_group "Generating set of a group") generates the entire group.

## Matrix representation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation_(geometry)&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Matrix representation")]

A translation is an [affine transformation](/wiki/Affine_transformation "Affine transformation") with *no* [fixed points](/wiki/Fixed_point_(mathematics) "Fixed point (mathematics)"). Matrix multiplications *always* have the [origin](/wiki/Origin_(mathematics) "Origin (mathematics)") as a fixed point. Nevertheless, there is a common [workaround](/wiki/Workaround "Workaround") using [homogeneous coordinates](/wiki/Homogeneous_coordinates "Homogeneous coordinates") to represent a translation of a [vector space](/wiki/Vector_space "Vector space") with [matrix multiplication](/wiki/Matrix_multiplication "Matrix multiplication"): Write the 3-dimensional vector ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {v} =(v_{x},v_{y},v_{z})}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0744af00f6b08dd2f26a2c76271389a3c25c5cb6) using 4 homogeneous coordinates as ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {v} =(v_{x},v_{y},v_{z},1)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/0a7aef15200ee6dc28cde107cdc09a604c362af7).[[2]](#cite_note-2)

To translate an object by a [vector](/wiki/Vector_(geometry) "Vector (geometry)") ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {v} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/35c1866e359fbfd2e0f606c725ba5cc37a5195d6), each homogeneous vector ![{\displaystyle \mathbf {p} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/dd73e3862cb92b016721b8c492eadb4e8a577527) (written in homogeneous coordinates) can be multiplied by this **translation matrix**:

:   ![{\displaystyle T_{\mathbf {v} }={\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0&v_{x}\\0&1&0&v_{y}\\0&0&1&v_{z}\\0&0&0&1\end{bmatrix}}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/f8291f8c9e148126f931b49fa4fa9a96827aceeb)

As shown below, the multiplication will give the expected result:

:   ![{\displaystyle T_{\mathbf {v} }\mathbf {p} ={\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0&v_{x}\\0&1&0&v_{y}\\0&0&1&v_{z}\\0&0&0&1\end{bmatrix}}{\begin{bmatrix}p_{x}\\p_{y}\\p_{z}\\1\end{bmatrix}}={\begin{bmatrix}p_{x}+v_{x}\\p_{y}+v_{y}\\p_{z}+v_{z}\\1\end{bmatrix}}=\mathbf {p} +\mathbf {v} }](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/a7bb4edd686c84393b22e27b4507f2e1ebc03aac)

The inverse of a translation matrix can be obtained by reversing the direction of the vector (in other words, it is an [M-matrix](/wiki/M-matrix "M-matrix")):

:   ![{\displaystyle T_{\mathbf {v} }^{-1}=T_{-\mathbf {v} }.\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/7ed2142aa1a48d83d35e67221cf40430956784ed)

Similarly, the product of translation matrices is given by adding the vectors:

:   ![{\displaystyle T_{\mathbf {v} }T_{\mathbf {w} }=T_{\mathbf {v} +\mathbf {w} }.\!}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/794b2b94e0ae0d7fb594270c932d6c031b06c1e1)

Because addition of vectors is [commutative](/wiki/Commutative "Commutative"), multiplication of translation matrices is therefore also commutative (unlike multiplication of arbitrary matrices).

## Translation of axes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation_(geometry)&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Translation of axes")]

Main article: [Translation of axes](/wiki/Translation_of_axes "Translation of axes")

While geometric translation is often viewed as an [active transformation](/wiki/Active_transformation "Active transformation") that changes the position of a geometric object, a similar result can be achieved by a [passive transformation](/wiki/Passive_transformation "Passive transformation") that moves the coordinate system itself but leaves the object fixed. The passive version of an active geometric translation is known as a *[translation of axes](/wiki/Translation_of_axes "Translation of axes")*.

## Translational symmetry

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation_(geometry)&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Translational symmetry")]

Main article: [Translational symmetry](/wiki/Translational_symmetry "Translational symmetry")

An object that looks the same before and after translation is said to have [translational symmetry](/wiki/Translational_symmetry "Translational symmetry"). A common example is a [periodic function](/wiki/Periodic_function "Periodic function"), which is an [eigenfunction](/wiki/Eigenfunction "Eigenfunction") of a translation operator.

## Translations of a graph

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation_(geometry)&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Translations of a graph")]

"Vertical translation" redirects here. For the concept in physics, see [Vertical separation](/wiki/Vertical_separation "Vertical separation").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/92/Translated_graph_of_a_function.png/330px-Translated_graph_of_a_function.png)](/wiki/File:Translated_graph_of_a_function.png)

Compared to the graph *y* = *f*(*x*), the graph *y* = *f*(*x* − *a*) has been translated horizontally by a, while the graph *y* = *f*(*x*) + b has been translated vertically by b.

The [graph](/wiki/Graph_of_a_function "Graph of a function") of a [real function](/wiki/Real_function "Real function") f, the set of points ⁠![{\displaystyle (x,f(x))}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/b21dd0c5c5815bc0516f679f631fd588ceb458d6)⁠, is often pictured in the [real coordinate plane](/wiki/Real_coordinate_plane "Real coordinate plane") with x as the horizontal coordinate and ⁠![{\displaystyle y=f(x)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/2311a6a75c54b0ea085a381ba472c31d59321514)⁠ as the vertical coordinate.

Starting from the graph of f, a **horizontal translation** means [composing](/wiki/Function_composition "Function composition") f with a function ⁠![{\displaystyle x\mapsto x-a}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5fb1a42aba777da68a7af2ecb9679a0226b4d5fb)⁠, for some constant number a, resulting in a graph consisting of points ⁠![{\displaystyle (x,f(x-a))}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/beb4406b0b71bc6c906c1c9a7e0cb9714e1cafc0)⁠. Each point ⁠![{\displaystyle (x,y)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/41cf50e4a314ca8e2c30964baa8d26e5be7a9386)⁠ of the original graph corresponds to the point ⁠![{\displaystyle (x+a,y)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/bbd627858d06c17459b4d8872bdfc36073bbbb3c)⁠ in the new graph, which pictorially results in a horizontal shift.

A **vertical translation** means composing the function ⁠![{\displaystyle y\mapsto y+b}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/fb5f7d080dd0d2e259e958cf8639c98083f7f867)⁠ with f, for some constant b, resulting in a graph consisting of the points ⁠![{\displaystyle {\bigl (}x,f(x)+b{\bigr )}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/d143bba84225ddebe9bc64cf4e8c4c088a59159d)⁠. Each point ⁠![{\displaystyle (x,y)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/41cf50e4a314ca8e2c30964baa8d26e5be7a9386)⁠ of the original graph corresponds to the point ⁠![{\displaystyle (x,y+b)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/83b67b30a359932520ea7a581fa95eb26e8eb97e)⁠ in the new graph, which pictorially results in a vertical shift.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

For example, taking the [quadratic function](/wiki/Quadratic_function "Quadratic function") ⁠![{\displaystyle y=x^{2}}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/ad1108c4c9ee8ac7de90b77f9bd27415b13b6bf1)⁠, whose graph is a [parabola](/wiki/Parabola "Parabola") with vertex at ⁠![{\displaystyle (0,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/5d630d3e781a53b0a3559ae7e5b45f9479a3141a)⁠, a horizontal translation 5 units to the right would be the new function ⁠![{\displaystyle y=(x-5)^{2}=x^{2}-10x+25}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/e4db1642234a168bc4f0611f7bcd52c8d19bdecf)⁠ whose vertex has coordinates ⁠![{\displaystyle (5,0)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/47a6520c29d84cc00fbe6235cbdcb0a8e79b95e8)⁠. A vertical translation 3 units upward would be the new function ⁠![{\displaystyle y=x^{2}+3}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/931e4a245570a2590951a53eec38296778a172b2)⁠ whose vertex has coordinates ⁠![{\displaystyle (0,3)}](https://wikimedia.org/api/rest_v1/media/math/render/svg/facd8877b12aa8477bf175fbfd33ce75a93955cd)⁠.

The [antiderivatives](/wiki/Antiderivative "Antiderivative") of a function all differ from each other by a [constant of integration](/wiki/Constant_of_integration "Constant of integration") and are therefore vertical translates of each other.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Translation_(geometry)&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Applications")]

For describing [vehicle dynamics](/wiki/Vehicle_dynamics "Vehicle dynamics") (or movement of any [rigid body](/wiki/Rigid_body "Rigid body")), including [ship dynamics](/wiki/Ship_motions "Ship motions") and [aircraft dynamics](/wiki/Aircraft_principal_axes "Aircraft principal axes"), it is common to use a mechanical model consisting of six [degrees of freedom](/wiki/Degrees_of_freedom_(mechanics) "Degrees of freedom (mechanics)"), which includes translations along three reference axes (as well as rotations about those three axes). These translations are often called [*surge*](/wiki/Ship_motions#Surge "Ship motions"), [*sway*](/wiki/Ship_motions#Sway "Ship motions"), and [*heave*](/wiki/Ship_motions#Heave "Ship motions").
